narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from our law school admissions process—it's a tough moment, and we're here to help you make sense of it. Your profile includes Year 1 school decile rankings in the third percentile, Year 3 in the second, a lower-middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 29, an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 52.6% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to this decision, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass on their initial attempt. The primary factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which fell well below the typical fifth-decile average and signaled weaker performance relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile, also in the lower third compared to the average fifth-decile, played a strong supporting role in that direction, highlighting a consistent academic challenge. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income stood out as a helpful influence, performing better than the dataset's average and nudging the prediction toward bar exam success. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 was close to the typical 3.21 average, providing a modest boost against failure, while your LSAT score, though below the usual 36.7, still contributed positively in its own way. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority of successful applicants, offering a tiny favorable push. Overall, your academic deciles were the biggest drivers toward rejection, outweighing the strengths in your background and scores when stacked against typical students who pass the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim higher than 29, bolstering your recent grades to climb those school deciles, and exploring programs that support students from similar income backgrounds. Many applicants in your position strengthen their profiles this way and succeed elsewhere—keep pushing, and we're rooting for your next steps.
